_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
condition: Management of Chest injury with fracture of Long bone
procedure_name: Management of Chest injury with fixation of Single Long bone
source_files: Management of Chest injury with fracture of Long bone_Medco.pdf
Management of Chest injury with fracture of Long bone_PPD.CPD.pdf
clinical_indications: Evidence of chest injury (e.g., rib fractures, sternal fracture, pulmonary contusion, hemothorax, pneumothorax, flail chest)
Evidence of single long bone fracture
Respiratory compromise (saturation <95% or <98% with oxygen, RR >30, abnormal CXR/auscultation)
Signs of high velocity accident / polytrauma
Hemodynamic instability (unresponsive hypotension SBP<60mm Hg, rapid exsanguination from chest tube, persistent hypotension)
Blunt Cardiac Injury (trauma to cardiac box, hemopericardium, elevated CVP, tachyarrhythmias, raised cardiac trop T & I)
Great Vessel Injury (widened mediastinum, loss of Aortic Knob Contour, shift of ET tube/trachea, first rib fracture, acute left sided hemothorax, retro-cardiac density)
Flail Chest (3 or more ribs fractured at two or more places, resulting in hypoventilation, poor pulmonary drainage, atelectasis)

preauth.label: Clinical Notes detailing the injury and need for surgery
Medico legal case report/ FIR copy of accident
X-ray/ CT report of fractured limb

claim.label: Post op X-ray film and report of chest

ppd_preauth.q: Do clinical notes detail the accident, signs & symptoms, indication for surgery and planned line of treatment?
Was the Medico legal report/ FIR copy of the accident submitted?
Did X-ray/ CT report suggest fracture of one long bone?
Did X-ray/ CT report suggest fracture of more than one long bone?

cpd_claim.q: Were the indoor case papers submitted?
Are the detailed Procedure/ Operation notes submitted?
Did the post op X-ray film show repair of chest injury and fixation with implant?
Was the Barcode of implant, if used submitted?
Is there a Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?

common_queries: CT chest report not suggestive of chest injury.
X-ray/CT report does not suggest fracture of a single long bone.
Clinical notes do not detail the accident, signs & symptoms, indication for surgery, or planned line of treatment.
Medico legal case report/FIR copy of the accident is missing or incomplete.
Post-operative X-ray film does not show repair of chest injury and fixation with implant.
Barcode of implant is missing, if an implant was used.

clinical_key_pointers: Secondary Survey (adequate exposure, penetrating injury, air leaking/sucking, abrasions/tenderness, paradoxical respiratory movements, crepitus/surgical emphysema, dullness/hyper resonance on percussion, reduced/absent air entry)
Emergency intubation, Intercostal tube drainage, Thoracotomy (initial management)
Rib fractures (most common chest injury; significance of 1-3 ribs fracture for brachial plexus/vascularity, 4-9 ribs fracture for liver/spleen/diaphragm/kidney/lung/bronchus/heart/pleura)
Always palpate the sternum, clavicle and spine
X-ray findings for bony thorax fractures, foreign bodies, lung fields (pneumothorax, hemothorax, lung contusion), mediastinum (widening, pneumomediastinum or shifting)
CT Chest Indications (saturation, CXR changes, respiratory rate, chest auscultation/palpation abnormality, high velocity accident / poly trauma)
Main Pillars of management (adequate analgesia, prevention for atelectasis, pulmonary toilet)
Flail Chest (when 3 or more ribs fractured at two or more places, unilateral or bilateral; results in hypoventilation, poor pulmonary drainage and atelectasis)
Sternal Fracture (conservative management, fixation if fractured segment unstable, may require ventilation)

Blunt Cardiac Injury (trauma to 'Cardiac Box', hemopericardium, hypotension, elevated CVP, decreased heart sounds/new murmur, new onset tachyarrhythmias, raised Cardiac trop T & I; echocardiography investigation of choice)
Great Vessel Injury (Aorta & branches, Pulmonary artery & veins, Vena cava & Azygos veins)

Surgical Emphysema (leakage of air into subcutaneous space, blotted appearance; history indicates side; if started from neck suspect airway injury; X-ray chest for pneumothorax; conservative management if no pneumothorax)
Penetrating Chest Trauma (assess hemodynamic stability; if unstable, emergency tube thoracostomy and echo; wound dressing and shift to OR for exploration if sucking wound present)

Emergency Thoracotomy Indications (unresponsive hypotension (SBP<60mm Hg), rapid exsanguination from indwelling chest tube (>1500ml), traumatic arrest with witnessed cardiac activity after penetrating thoracic injuries, persistent hypotension (SBP <60 mm Hg) with diagnosed cardiac tamponade/air embolism)
surgical_options: Thoracotomy (for unstable chest wall, other procedures, inability to wean from ventilator)
Endovascular stenting / Open repair for Great Vessel Injury
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Primary Survey and resuscitation; Emergency intubation, Intercostal tube drainage, Thoracotomy (as initial management steps).
additional_information.post_op_care: Adequate Analgesia (NSAIDs, Intravenous Opiates, Epidural, Intercostal Rib Block); Prevention for Atelectasis (Incentive Spirometry); Pulmonary Toilet (Chest Physiotherapy and coughing out Sputum); Lung protective ventilation strategies with adequate PEEP and low tidal volume (for Flail Chest management).
additional_information.fitness_criteria: Hemodynamically stable (as a decision point for further evaluation)
additional_information.comorbidity_management: Age >65 with even a single rib fracture should be admitted and observed.
